risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Using the random number generator in the statistical package Stata (version 9), and after stratification by geographical block (the administrative unit above the village), one author (ED) randomly selected 30 of the 134 study villages to receive intervention A and 30 to receive intervention B." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated random allocation sequence with stratification, which is an appropriate random method for sequence generation.

Quote: "Using the random number generator in the statistical package Stata (version 9), and after stratification by geographical block (the administrative unit above the village), one author (ED) randomly selected 30 of the 134 study villages to receive intervention A and 30 to receive intervention B." Comment: This describes sequence generation and who performed allocation, but it does not explicitly describe any allocation concealment mechanism (for example, central allocation or sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed envelopes). In this cluster trial, the report also does not clarify any concealment procedure before recruitment/selection, so foreknowledge by those enrolling participants cannot be ruled out or confirmed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Naturally, given the context and the intervention, the allocation of villages to treatment or control was not blind." Quote: "The study was not blind from the point of view of the villagers, who might have been motivated to attend the camp to ensure that Seva Mandir would not cancel the programme." Comment: Participants and personnel delivering the intervention were aware of assignment because villages either received camps, camps plus incentives, or no additional intervention. The main outcomes (receipt of immunisations, complete immunisation) are behaviour-dependent and plausibly influenced by knowledge of allocation, especially where incentives were offered; the paper explicitly notes villagers' motivation could be affected by lack of blinding.

Quote: "Naturally, given the context and the intervention, the allocation of villages to treatment or control was not blind." "Most households in the areas did not have immunisation cards so we ascertained the immunisation rate during interviews with the mother." "Both surveys were blind: interviewers did not know which villages belonged to which intervention (or control) group." "Self report can be influenced by recall bias (mothers, who are often illiterate, might not remember) and potentially by social desirability bias, which might be affected by their intervention group." Comment: Outcomes were assessed by both patient/parent self-report (mother interviewed about child immunisation status) and independent assessors/field officers for some objective checks (BCG scar, logbook data). However, by the study's own report, participants/villagers were not blinded, and the main outcome relied on maternal self-report, which is subjective and explicitly noted as potentially affected by intervention group. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of an unblinded self-reported outcome makes detection bias high risk overall, even though interviewers were blinded and some objective outcomes were also collected.

Quote: "The final sample in the household surveys included 2188 children aged 1-3 years at the end point from 2898 households in five groups of villages: 74 control villages (n=860 children), 30 villages in intervention group A (n=379), 30 in intervention group B (n=382), 27 neighbouring an intervention group A village (n=265), and 26 neighbouring an intervention group B village (n=302) (fig 1)." Quote: "Within each village, a household census was conducted, and 30 households containing children aged 0-5 years were randomly selected with a random number generator to be part of the sample. The same households were surveyed again at the end point." Comment: The paper reports endpoint sample sizes by arm, but does not report the corresponding numbers randomized/selected and successfully followed up for the analysed primary end-point cohort (children aged 1-3 years at endpoint) by arm, nor reasons for any post-randomization household/child loss. Because per-arm attrition on the analysed outcome cannot be calculated from the reported data, attrition bias is unclear rather than high.
